---
title: "Forecasting Singapore's Total Live Births and Total Fertility Rate: The Role of the Chinese Zodiac Dragon Year Effect"
author: "Kiran Shankaran"
date: "8 June 2026"
output:
  pdf_document:
    number_sections: false
    fig_caption: true
geometry: margin=1in
fontsize: 11pt
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{setspace}
  - \onehalfspacing
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup[table]{width=\textwidth}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# warnings, set default figure dimensions and centre alignment.
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
                      fig.width = 7, fig.height = 4, fig.align = "center")
```

```{r load-data, include=FALSE}
# Load pre-computed analysis results. These RDS files are produced by 
# running EDA.R and Analysis.R first.

kpss_table                <- readRDS("kpss_table.rds")
kpss_d2                   <- readRDS("kpss_d2.rds")
acf_pacf_table            <- readRDS("acf_pacf_table.rds")
acf_pacf_table_first_diff <- readRDS("acf_pacf_table_first_diff.rds")

tfr_results <- readRDS("tfr_results.rds")
tlb_results <- readRDS("tlb_results.rds")
tfr_table   <- readRDS("tfr_table.rds")
tlb_table   <- readRDS("tlb_table.rds")
best_tfr    <- readRDS("best_tfr.rds")
best_tlb    <- readRDS("best_tlb.rds")
```

# Abstract

This study investigates whether the Chinese zodiac Dragon Year effect is still
useful for forecasting Singapore’s Total Live Births (TLB) and Total Fertility
Rate (TFR). Previous research has shown that Chinese-majority populations tend
to record higher births in Dragon years and lower births in Tiger years, but the
2024 Dragon Year did not produce an obvious increase in either series. Annual
data from 1960 to 2024 were obtained from the Singapore Department of Statistics
(SingStat Table M810091) and split into a 1960–2012 training period and a
2013–2024 test period. For each series, thirteen candidate models were tested,
including non-seasonal ARIMA models and SARIMA models with a 12-year seasonal
period based on the zodiac cycle. Model viability was assessed using the
Ljung-Box test on residuals at lag 24, and viable models were compared using
out-of-sample RMSE. For TLB, the best model was SARIMA(1,1,1)(0,1,1)[12], which
reduced test-set RMSE by 57% compared with the best viable non-seasonal
alternative. This suggests that the 12-year seasonal structure is useful for
forecasting absolute birth counts. For TFR, ARIMA(13,2,2) slightly outperformed
all SARIMA candidates, suggesting the zodiac effect is weaker once births are
expressed as a population-adjusted rate. The 2024 result also suggests this
historical pattern should not be treated as permanent, because recent fertility
behaviour may no longer follow the same cultural timing as earlier cohorts did
in the past.

GitHub repository: <https://github.com/kiranshank12519/Time-Series-Project-Singapore-Births-Forecast>

---

# 1. Introduction and Literature Review

Singapore has experienced a major decline in fertility over the last several
decades. The Total Fertility Rate (TFR), which measures the average number of
children a woman would have over her lifetime under current fertility rates,
fell from about 5.76 in 1960 to 0.97 in 2024. Total Live Births (TLB) also
declined from 61,775 to 33,703 over the same period (Singapore Department of
Statistics, 2024). This matters because low fertility contributes to population
ageing and a smaller future working-age population. Singapore’s old-age support
ratio has already fallen from 5.7 in 2015 to 3.3 in 2025, and is projected to
fall further to 2.7 by 2030, meaning fewer working-age residents will be
supporting each older resident (Ministry of Manpower, 2025). By 2030, around one
in four Singaporeans are also expected to be aged 65 or above (Ministry of
Health, 2024). Accurate forecasting of births is therefore useful for
demographic planning, labour-force planning and future fertility-related policy.

Singapore’s fertility decline has occurred despite major policy changes. Chen,
Yip and Yap (2018) describe Singapore’s shift from anti-natalist policy during
1965–1986 to pro-natalist policy from 1987 onwards, including the slogan “Three
or more, if you can afford it”. This was later repackaged as the Marriage and
Parenthood Package in 2001 and expanded in 2004, 2008, 2013 and 2015 (Chen, Yip
and Yap, 2018). However, TFR has remained below replacement level. Chen, Yip and
Yap (2018) argue that one reason for this is that policy incentives have not
always targeted the groups with the strongest effect on fertility, especially
single women aged 20–29 and married childless women aged 30–34.

Alongside the long-term decline, Singapore’s birth data may also contain a
12-year cycle linked to the Chinese zodiac calendar. This is relevant because
Singapore has a large ethnic Chinese population. In the 2020 Census, 74.3% of
Singapore residents were Chinese, compared with 13.5% Malay, 9.0% Indian and
3.2% from other ethnic groups (Singapore Department of Statistics, 2020). Since
the Dragon is traditionally viewed as an auspicious zodiac sign, some Chinese
families may prefer to have children in Dragon years, while Tiger years may be
viewed less favourably. Agarwal et al. (2021) found that Chinese births in
Singapore increased by about 9.7% in Dragon years and fell by a similar amount
in Tiger years. Since the zodiac cycle repeats every 12 years, this creates a
possible periodic pattern in birth data. This effect is expected to be clearer
in Total Live Births than in TFR because TLB measures actual birth counts, while
TFR is adjusted for the female population. This provides the main reason for
testing whether a 12-year seasonal model improves forecasts for Singapore’s
birth data.

The motivation for this study is that the 2024 Dragon Year did not follow the
historical pattern. Instead of producing an obvious increase in births, TLB fell
to 33,703, the lowest value in the series, while TFR remained at 0.97 (Singapore
Department of Statistics, 2024). This is important because Singapore has a large
ethnic Chinese population, and the Dragon Year effect documented by Agarwal et
al. (2021) was found specifically in Chinese births. Therefore, if this cultural
timing effect is weakening, a forecasting model that assumes the 12-year pattern
will continue may overestimate future births. Initial EDA also supported testing
the zodiac cycle more directly, as the non-seasonal ARIMA(0,1,1) model produced
a mostly flat TLB forecast and the residual ACF showed a marginal spike at lag
12. The research question of this report is: does the historical Chinese zodiac
Dragon Year effect remain a useful forecasting signal for Singapore’s Total Live
Births and Total Fertility Rate, given that the 2024 Dragon Year produced no
observable birth boost? To answer this, the report compares SARIMA models with
an explicit 12-year seasonal period against non-seasonal ARIMA models, using
residual diagnostics and out-of-sample RMSE to assess model performance.

# 3. Methods

## 3.1 Data

The analysis used annual observations on Singapore's Total Fertility Rate (TFR,
in births per female) and Total Live Births (TLB, in counts) for the period 1960
to 2024. This data was obtained from the Singapore Department of Statistics
(SingStat Table M810091). The source file was provided in wide format with years
as columns running from 2025 (left) to 1960 (right), and missing values encoded
as the string "na". Standard preparation was applied: indented row labels were
trimmed of leading whitespace, year order was reversed to chronological, missing
values were converted to R's `NA`, and the incomplete 2025 column was excluded.

As the purpose of this project is to identify a model and forecast TBL and TFR,
the data was split into a training set covering 1960 to 2012 (53 observations)
and a test set covering 2013 to 2024 (12 observations). All models were
estimated on the training set, and forecast accuracy was evaluated against the
test set. All modelling was performed in R using the base `stats` package and
the `tseries` package for stationarity testing.

## 3.2 Transformation and Stationary Testing

Before fitting ARIMA and SARIMA models, the training series needed to be
transformed into a stationary form. This is important because ARIMA-type models
assume that the underlying dependence structure of the series is stable over
time. If a series still contains a strong trend or changing variance, the
ACF/PACF plots and fitted ARIMA terms can be misleading, as they may reflect the
trend rather than the actual autocorrelation structure. The raw TFR and TLB
series both showed clear downward trends, and the variation in the series also
appeared to change over time. A log transformation was therefore applied first
to help stabilise the variance before differencing. After this, two possible
transformation approaches were tested.

Route 1 (seasonal). The first approach used a log transformation, followed by
seasonal differencing at lag 12 and then first-order regular differencing. The
lag 12 seasonal difference was used because the Chinese zodiac cycle repeats
every 12 years, and previous literature has found a Dragon Year effect in
Singapore births (Agarwal et al., 2021). This route supports SARIMA models of
the form $(p, 1, q)(P, 1, Q)_{12}$.

Route 2 (non-seasonal). The second approach used a log transformation followed
by second-order regular differencing. This route does not directly assume a
12-year zodiac cycle. Instead, it allows non-seasonal ARIMA models of the form
ARIMA$(p, 2, q)$ to capture any remaining autocorrelation through higher AR or
MA orders.

The Kwiatkowski–Phillips–Schmidt–Shin (KPSS) test was then used to check whether
each transformation route had made the series stationary (Kwiatkowski et al.,
1992). The KPSS test has a null hypothesis that the series is stationary, so a
p-value below 0.05 was taken as evidence that the series was still
non-stationary. The full test specification is given in Appendix A.4. The KPSS
test was applied after each transformation stage for both TFR and TLB, with the
results shown in Table 1. Both routes achieved stationarity at the 5% level for
both series. For this reason, both were kept for model testing. This allowed the
seasonal SARIMA approach to be compared with a non-seasonal ARIMA alternative,
rather than assuming from the start that the zodiac cycle had to be included.

```{r kpss-table, echo=FALSE}
kpss_combined <- rbind(kpss_table, kpss_d2)
knitr::kable(kpss_combined,
             caption = "KPSS stationarity test results across transformation stages. The null hypothesis is level stationarity; p < 0.05 indicates rejection of stationarity. p-values below 0.01 and above 0.10 are reported at those bounds.",
             col.names = c("KPSS Statistic", "p-value"),
             align = "rr")
```

## 3.3 Non-seasonal ARIMA Models

A non-seasonal ARIMA$(p,d,q)$ model was used as one way to model the transformed
TFR and TLB series. The general form of the model is given in Appendix A.1,
where $p$ is the autoregressive order, $d$ is the differencing order, and $q$ is
the moving average order.

For the non-seasonal route, the main models tested used $d = 2$, because the
log-transformed series became stationary after second-order differencing. The
models ARIMA$(13,2,2)$ and ARIMA$(12,2,3)$ were included to allow the
non-seasonal model to capture autocorrelation around lag 12 without using an
explicit seasonal term. Four additional $d = 1$ models were also tested for
comparison: ARIMA$(0,1,13)$, ARIMA$(13,1,0)$, ARIMA$(0,1,11)$, and
ARIMA$(12,1,1)$. These were chosen because the ACF/PACF plots suggested
structure around lag 12, so higher AR or MA orders were needed if seasonality
was not modelled directly.

## 3.4 Seasonal ARIMA Models

SARIMA models were also tested because the Chinese zodiac cycle repeats every 12
years. The general SARIMA$(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s$ form is given in Appendix A.2. In
this study, the seasonal period was set as $s = 12$ to represent the possible
Dragon Year cycle. The SARIMA candidates used $d = 1$ and $D = 1$, based on the
seasonal transformation route described earlier. Two main seasonal structures
were tested. The first used a seasonal moving average term at lag 12,
SARIMA$(p,1,q)(0,1,1){12}$, and the second used a seasonal autoregressive term
at lag 12, SARIMA$(p,1,q)(1,1,0){12}$. Within these models, the non-seasonal $p$
and $q$ values were kept small, usually 0 or 1, to avoid making the models too
complex for the 53-observation training set. One larger seasonal model,
SARIMA$(1,1,1)(1,1,1)_{12}$, was also included to check whether using both
seasonal AR and seasonal MA terms improved the fit. All candidate models were
fitted to the log-transformed training data using the arima() function in R.
Forecasts were produced on the log scale and then back-transformed using the
exponential function so that forecast accuracy could be measured on the original
TFR and TLB scales.

## 3.5 Residual Diagnostics

After fitting each model, the residuals were checked to see whether the model
had captured the main time-series structure. This was done using the residual
ACF up to lag 24, a normal Q-Q plot, and the Ljung-Box test. The Ljung-Box test
was applied at lag 24, with the degrees of freedom adjusted for the number of
fitted model parameters. The test checks whether the residual autocorrelations
are jointly equal to zero. A model was treated as viable if the Ljung-Box
p-value was greater than 0.05, meaning there was not enough evidence of
remaining autocorrelation in the residuals.Models that failed the Ljung-Box test
were excluded from the forecast comparison. This was done because a low AIC or
RMSE is not enough if the residuals still contain clear autocorrelation. In that
case, the model has not fully captured the structure in the series.

## 3.6 Forecast Evaluation

Each viable model was used to forecast the 2013–2024 test period. Forecast
accuracy was measured using RMSE on the original scale, so TFR errors were
measured in births per woman and TLB errors were measured in number of births.
AIC and AICc were also reported to compare in-sample fit, but they were not used
as the final selection criterion. AICc was included because the training sample
only contained 53 observations, and some of the candidate models had a
relatively large number of parameters.

Prediction intervals were also calculated for each forecast. The 80% and 95%
intervals were first constructed on the log scale using the forecast standard
errors from arima(), then back-transformed to the original scale. These
intervals were included in the forecast plots to show the uncertainty around
each model’s point forecasts. The final model for each series was chosen as the
viable model with the lowest test-set RMSE. This meant that models first had to
pass the residual white-noise check, and were then ranked based on out-of-sample
forecast accuracy.

# 4. Results

## 4.1 Series Behaviour

Figures 1 and 2 show Singapore’s TFR and TLB from 1960 to 2024. Dragon years are
marked with red circles and Tiger years with blue triangles. The vertical dashed
line at 2012 separates the training data from the 2013–2024 test period.

```{r fig1, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Singapore Total Fertility Rate, 1960-2024. Red circles mark Chinese zodiac Dragon years; blue triangles mark Tiger years. The dashed vertical line at 2012 marks the train/test boundary."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig1_tfr_raw.png")
```

```{r fig2, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Singapore Total Live Births, 1960-2024. Red circles mark Chinese zodiac Dragon years; blue triangles mark Tiger years. The dashed vertical line at 2012 marks the train/test boundary."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig2_tlb_raw.png")
```

Both series show a clear long-term decline. TFR fell from about 5.76 in 1960 to
0.97 in 2024, while TLB fell from about 61,700 births to 33,700 births over the
same period. The decline was steepest from 1960 to the early 1980s. After this,
both series became more stable, although they still continued to trend downward.

The Dragon-Tiger pattern is easier to see in TLB than in TFR. Several Dragon
years line up with local increases in births, especially 1988 and 2000. For
example, the 1988 Dragon year had about 53,000 births, compared with about
38,000 births in the nearby Tiger years of 1986 and 1998. Tiger years also tend
to line up with local lows in the birth count. However, 2024 does not follow
this earlier pattern. Even though 2024 was a Dragon year, TLB fell to 33,703,
the lowest value in the series, with no clear Dragon-year increase. The pattern
is less obvious in TFR. This makes sense because TFR is a rate adjusted for the
female population, while the zodiac effect is expected to affect the actual
number of births more directly. This is why the Dragon Year effect is expected
to be more visible in TLB than in TFR.

## 4.2 Transformations and Stationarity  

Both raw series were non-stationary based on the KPSS test. For TFR, the KPSS
statistic was 1.0706 with $p = 0.0100$, and for TLB the statistic was 0.6236
with $p = 0.0205$. Since both $p$-values were below 0.05, level stationarity was
rejected for both series, as shown in Table 1. Applying the log transformation
by itself did not make either series stationary. Figures 3 and 4 show the
transformation process for TFR and TLB, starting with the raw series, then the
log-transformed series, then the series after seasonal differencing at lag 12,
and finally after first-order regular differencing.

```{r fig3, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="TFR transformation stages. Top left: raw TFR (training). Top right: log-transformed TFR. Bottom left: log(TFR) after seasonal differencing at lag 12. Bottom right: log(TFR) after seasonal and first-order regular differencing."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig5_tfr_transformations.png")
```

```{r fig4, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="TLB transformation stages. Top left: raw TLB (training). Top right: log-transformed TLB. Bottom left: log(TLB) after seasonal differencing at lag 12. Bottom right: log(TLB) after seasonal and first-order regular differencing."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig6_tlb_transformations.png")
```

The final panels in Figures 3 and 4 show that the transformed series behave much
more like stationary series. After both seasonal and regular differencing, the
values fluctuate around zero, with no clear trend or obvious change in variance.
This was also supported by the KPSS results, with $p \geq 0.10$ for both final
transformed series. The KPSS statistics were 0.1360 for TFR and 0.1227 for TLB.
The alternative non-seasonal route, using a log transformation followed by
second-order regular differencing, also achieved stationarity for both series
with $p \geq 0.10$. Therefore, both transformation routes were considered
suitable for model fitting.

## 4.3 Model Identification

To choose the candidate model orders, the ACF and PACF were calculated for the
log-transformed and first-differenced TFR and TLB series. The plots were checked
up to lag 36 so that three full 12-year zodiac cycles could be inspected. These
plots are shown in Figures 5 and 6.

```{r fig5, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="ACF (left) and PACF (right) of log-transformed TFR after first-order regular differencing, training period 1960-2012. Dashed blue lines indicate $\\pm 2/\\sqrt{n}$ significance bounds."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig7_tfr_acf_pacf_first_diff.png")
```

```{r fig6, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="ACF (left) and PACF (right) of log-transformed TLB after first-order regular differencing, training period 1960-2012. Dashed blue lines indicate $\\pm 2/\\sqrt{n}$ significance bounds."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig8_tlb_acf_pacf_first_diff.png")
```

For both series, the main feature is a clear positive spike at lag 12 in the ACF
and PACF. The spike is approximately 0.34 for TFR and 0.37 for TLB, which is
above the significance bound of $\pm 0.273$. This suggests that there is still
important structure at the 12-year lag after first differencing. The PACF also
shows a negative spike at lag 13 for both series, which further supports the
possibility of a 12-year pattern. Based on this, two types of candidate models
were tested. The first group used SARIMA models with an explicit seasonal term
at lag 12, so the zodiac cycle was modelled directly. The second group used
higher-order non-seasonal ARIMA models, where the AR or MA order was large
enough to capture structure around lag 12 without adding a seasonal term. This
allowed the seasonal and non-seasonal approaches to be compared rather than
assuming that the zodiac effect had to be included.

## 4.4 Fitted Models and Diagnostics

Thirteen candidate models were fitted for each series. Tables 2 and 3 compare
the models for TFR and TLB, using AIC, AICc, the Ljung-Box $p$-value, viability
status and test-set RMSE.

```{r tfr-table, echo=FALSE}
knitr::kable(tfr_table,
             caption = "TFR candidate model comparison. Viability is determined by the Ljung-Box test on residuals at lag 24 (p > 0.05). RMSE is computed on the 2013-2024 test set after back-transformation from the log scale.",
             align = "lrrrlr")
```

```{r tlb-table, echo=FALSE}
knitr::kable(tlb_table,
             caption = "TLB candidate model comparison. Viability is determined by the Ljung-Box test on residuals at lag 24 (p > 0.05). RMSE is computed on the 2013-2024 test set after back-transformation from the log scale.",
             align = "lrrrlr")
```

For TFR, nine of the thirteen models passed the Ljung-Box white-noise check. The
non-seasonal $d = 1$ models did not perform well, with all four producing
residuals that still had significant autocorrelation. Their Ljung-Box $p$-values
ranged from 0.0042 to 0.0231, so they were excluded from the final forecast
comparison. ARIMA$(12,1,1)$ also failed to converge during model fitting. The
viable TFR models were therefore the two $d = 2$ ARIMA models and the seven
SARIMA models. The best viable model was ARIMA$(13,2,2)$, with RMSE 0.0862. The
best SARIMA model, SARIMA$(1,1,1)(1,1,0)_{12}$, was close behind with RMSE
0.0965.

For TLB, seven of the thirteen models passed the Ljung-Box white-noise check.
Three of the four $d = 1$ non-seasonal models failed the Ljung-Box test, while
ARIMA$(0,1,13)$ passed and was kept for comparison. ARIMA$(13,2,2)$ also passed,
although its Ljung-Box $p$-value of 0.0928 was relatively close to the 5%
threshold. Two SARIMA models with seasonal AR terms failed the white-noise
check. The best viable TLB model was SARIMA$(1,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}$, with RMSE
2,730. This was much better than the best viable non-seasonal
model, ARIMA$(0,1,13)$, which had RMSE 6,386. Residual
diagnostics for the selected TFR and TLB models are shown in Figures 7 and 8.

```{r fig7, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Residual diagnostics for the best viable TFR model, ARIMA(13,2,2). Left: residual ACF up to lag 24 with significance bounds. Right: normal Q-Q plot."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig5_tfr_residuals.png")
```

```{r fig8, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Residual diagnostics for the best viable TLB model, SARIMA(1,1,1)(0,1,1)[12]. Left: residual ACF up to lag 24 with significance bounds. Right: normal Q-Q plot."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig6_tlb_residuals.png")
```

The TFR residual diagnostics look good overall. The residual ACF does not show
any significant autocorrelation, and the Q-Q plot stays close to the reference
line, with only small differences in the tails. This suggests that
ARIMA$(13,2,2)$ has captured the main structure in the TFR series. The TLB
residual diagnostics are slightly weaker. The residual ACF shows a borderline
negative spike around lag 4, and the Q-Q plot has some mild departures from
normality in the tails. However, the Ljung-Box test still does not reject the
white-noise assumption at the 5% level. This suggests that
SARIMA$(1,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}$ captures most of the autocorrelation in the TLB
series, although some minor residual structure may still remain.

## 4.5 Forecast Performance

The selected models were evaluated by comparing their forecasts with the actual
2013--2024 test data. This makes it possible to see how closely the models
followed the observed values, especially around the 2024 Dragon Year. Figures 9
and 10 show the forecasts for TFR and TLB, with the 80% and 95% prediction
intervals included.

```{r fig9, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="TFR forecast for 2013-2024 from ARIMA(13,2,2). Black line: training data. Grey line: actual test data. Red dashed line: point forecast. Shaded bands: 80% (darker) and 95% (lighter) prediction intervals."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig7_tfr_forecast.png")
```

```{r fig10, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="TLB forecast for 2013-2024 from SARIMA(1,1,1)(0,1,1)[12]. Black line: training data. Grey line: actual test data. Red dashed line: point forecast. Shaded bands: 80% (darker) and 95% (lighter) prediction intervals."}
knitr::include_graphics("figures/fig8_tlb_forecast.png")
```

The TFR forecast in Figure 9 follows the actual 2013--2024 test data fairly
closely. The point forecast stays within about 0.10 of the observed TFR values
across the test period, and all twelve actual observations fall inside the 95%
prediction interval. This suggests that ARIMA$(13,2,2)$ captures both the
continued downward trend and the size of the decline reasonably well.

The TLB forecast in Figure 10 is less straightforward. From 2013 to 2022, the
point forecast generally underpredicts the actual number of births by about
1,500--3,000 births per year, although the actual values still fall within the
95% prediction interval. In 2024, the SARIMA model forecasts an increase because
it carries forward the historical 12-year Dragon Year pattern. However, the
actual 2024 value was 33,703, which was the lowest value in the full series.
This means the model expected some Dragon Year increase, but this did not occur
in the observed data. Even so, the actual value still remained inside the wider
95% prediction interval. 

The seasonal model performed much better for TLB. SARIMA$(1,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}$
had a test-set RMSE of 2,730, compared with 6,386 for the best viable
non-seasonal model. This is a reduction of about 57%. For TFR, the result was
different. The non-seasonal ARIMA$(13,2,2)$ model had the lowest RMSE of 0.0862,
while the best SARIMA model had RMSE 0.0965. This means the seasonal structure
helped much more for TLB than for TFR.

# 5. Discussion

The results answer the research question in a mixed way. The 12-year zodiac
structure improved forecasts for Total Live Births, but it did not improve
forecasts for Total Fertility Rate. For TLB, the selected
SARIMA$(1,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}$ model had a much lower RMSE than the best viable
non-seasonal model, showing that the seasonal structure added useful
information. For TFR, the best model was instead ARIMA$(13,2,2)$, with the
SARIMA alternatives performing slightly worse. This suggests that the Dragon
Year effect is more useful for forecasting birth counts than fertility rates.

This difference is reasonable because the zodiac effect is mainly a timing
effect in actual births. Agarwal et al. (2021) found that Chinese births in
Singapore increased in Dragon years, so the effect should appear more directly
in TLB. TFR is adjusted for the female population, so the same birth timing
effect is less visible once converted into a rate. This explains why the
seasonal model helped TLB but did not clearly improve TFR. The 2024 result is
the most important qualification to this finding. Figure 10 shows that the
selected TLB model carried the historical Dragon Year pattern forward and
expected an increase in 2024. Instead, actual TLB fell to 33,703, the lowest
value in the series. The actual value was still inside the 95% prediction
interval, so the model allowed for uncertainty, but the point forecast was wrong
in direction. This suggests that the historical Dragon Year pattern was present
in the training data, but may not be as reliable in the most recent period.

There are also limitations in the modelling. First, although the selected TLB
model passed the Ljung-Box test, the residual diagnostics in Figure 8 still show
some minor remaining structure. This means the model captured most of the
autocorrelation, but not all of it. Second, the training set only had 53
observations, which limits how complex the models can be before overfitting
becomes a risk. Third, the analysis is univariate, so it does not include other
fertility drivers such as housing costs, labour market conditions, migration, or
policy changes. This matters because Chen, Yip and Yap (2018) show that
Singapore’s fertility is affected by policy design and demographic subgroups,
not only by past birth patterns.

Overall, the results suggest that the Dragon Year cycle is useful for explaining
historical variation in TLB, but it should not be treated as a permanent
forecasting rule. A SARIMA model trained on older data can capture the past
cycle, but may overestimate future births if the cultural behaviour behind the
effect weakens. Future work could test models with policy, economic or
demographic variables, or use rolling forecasts so that recent changes,
including the weak 2024 Dragon Year effect, have more influence on future
predictions.

# 6. Conclusion

This study tested whether the Chinese zodiac Dragon Year effect is still useful
for forecasting Singapore’s Total Live Births and Total Fertility Rate. Thirteen
ARIMA and SARIMA models were fitted for each series using the 1960--2012
training data and then tested against the 2013--2024 observations. Models first
had to pass the Ljung-Box white-noise check before being compared using test-set
RMSE. The results were different for the two series. For TLB, the best model was
SARIMA$(1,1,1)(0,1,1)_{12}$, with an RMSE of 2,730, compared with 6,386 for the
best viable non-seasonal model. This shows that the 12-year seasonal structure
helped forecast total birth counts. For TFR, the best model was ARIMA$(13,2,2)$,
with RMSE 0.0862 compared with 0.0965 for the best SARIMA model. This suggests
that the zodiac cycle is less useful once births are expressed as a fertility
rate rather than as an absolute count. The main caution is the 2024 result.
Although the seasonal TLB model captured the historical Dragon Year pattern, its
2024 point forecast expected an increase that did not occur. Actual TLB instead
fell to 33,703, although Figure 10 shows it still appears to fall within the
wider 95% prediction interval. Overall, the Dragon Year effect appears useful
for modelling historical TLB, but it should not be treated as a fixed pattern
that will always continue. Future work could improve the analysis by including
policy, economic or demographic variables, or by using forecasting methods that
give more weight to recent data.

# References

Agarwal, S., Qian, W., Sing, T. F. and Tan, P. (2021). Fortunes of the Dragons:
Cohort effects of zodiac superstitions on educational investments. *Journal of
Development Economics*, 152, 102696.

Chen, M., Yip, P. S. F. and Yap, M. T. (2018). Identifying the most influential
groups in determining Singapore's fertility. *Journal of Social Policy*, 47(1),
139--160. doi:10.1017/S0047279417000241

Kwiatkowski, D., Phillips, P. C. B., Schmidt, P. and Shin, Y. (1992). Testing
the null hypothesis of stationarity against the alternative of a unit root: How
sure are we that economic time series have a unit root? *Journal of
Econometrics*, 54(1--3), 159--178.

Ljung, G. M. and Box, G. E. P. (1978). On a measure of lack of fit in time
series models. *Biometrika*, 65(2), 297--303.

Ministry of Manpower. (2025). *Labour Force in Singapore 2024*. Available at: https://stats.mom.gov.sg/Pages/Labour-Force-In-Singapore-2024.aspx

National Population and Talent Division. (2025). *Longevity*. Available at: https://www.population.gov.sg/our-population/population-trends/longevity/

Shair, S. N., Shaadan, N., Amirudin, N. A. B. M. and A'kashalf, N. A. M. (2024).
Predictions of Malaysia age-specific fertility rates using the Lee-Carter and
the functional data approaches. *AIUB Journal of Science and Engineering*,
23(1), 71--78. doi:10.53799/ajse.v23i1.1024

Singapore Department of Statistics. (2020). *Census of Population 2020
Statistical Release 1: Demographic Characteristics, Education, Language and
Religion*. Singapore Department of Statistics.

Singapore Department of Statistics. (2024). *Table M810091: Births and Fertility
Rates, Annual*. SingStat Table Builder. Available at:
https://tablebuilder.singstat.gov.sg/

Vanella, P. (2024). The total fertility rate in Germany until 2040: A stochastic
principal components projection based on age-specific fertility rates. *Hannover
Economic Papers*, 579. \newpage

# Statistical Appendix

## A.1 ARIMA$(p, d, q)$ Model

An ARIMA$(p, d, q)$ model applied to a time series $x_t$ is written as

$$
\phi(B)\nabla^d x_t = \theta(B)w_t
$$

where $B$ is the backshift operator, so that $Bx_t = x_{t-1}$, and $\nabla^d = (1 - B)^d$ is the differencing operator of order $d$. The autoregressive polynomial is

$$
\phi(B) = 1 - \phi_1B - \phi_2B^2 - \cdots - \phi_pB^p,
$$

and the moving average polynomial is

$$
\theta(B) = 1 + \theta_1B + \theta_2B^2 + \cdots + \theta_qB^q.
$$

Here, $p$ is the autoregressive order, $d$ is the differencing order, $q$ is the moving average order, and $w_t$ is a white noise error term, usually written as $w_t \sim N(0,\sigma^2)$.

## A.2 Seasonal ARIMA (SARIMA) Model

A SARIMA$(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_s$ model extends ARIMA by adding seasonal autoregressive, differencing, and moving average terms at seasonal lag $s$:

$$
\Phi(B^s)\phi(B)\nabla_s^D\nabla^d x_t = \Theta(B^s)\theta(B)w_t
$$

where $\Phi(B^s)$ is the seasonal autoregressive polynomial, $\Theta(B^s)$ is the seasonal moving average polynomial, and

$$
\nabla_s^D = (1 - B^s)^D
$$

is the seasonal differencing operator. In this study, $s = 12$ was used to represent the proposed 12-year Chinese zodiac cycle.

## A.3 Autocorrelation and Partial Autocorrelation Functions

The sample autocorrelation at lag $k$ is

$$
r_k = \frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})(x_{t-k} - \bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^{n}(x_t - \bar{x})^2}.
$$

Approximate significance bounds under $H_0: \rho_k = 0$ are plotted at

$$
\pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}.
$$

The partial autocorrelation at lag $k$ measures the correlation between $x_t$ and $x_{t-k}$ after removing the linear effects of the observations in between.

## A.4 KPSS Test for Stationarity

The Kwiatkowski--Phillips--Schmidt--Shin (KPSS) test was used to check stationarity (Kwiatkowski et al., 1992). The null hypothesis is that the series is stationary, while the alternative is that the series is non-stationary. Therefore, a $p$-value below 0.05 was treated as evidence that the series was still non-stationary and required further transformation or differencing. In this study, the KPSS test was applied using the level stationarity null hypothesis.

## A.5 Ljung-Box Test for White Noise Residuals

The Ljung-Box test was used to check whether the residuals from each fitted model behaved like white noise. The test statistic is

$$
Q = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{h}\frac{\hat{r}_k^2}{n-k},
$$

where $n$ is the sample size, $h$ is the number of lags tested, and $\hat{r}_k$ is the residual autocorrelation at lag $k$.

The null hypothesis is that the first $h$ residual autocorrelations are jointly equal to zero. For an ARIMA$(p,d,q)$ model, the degrees of freedom are adjusted by the fitted AR and MA parameters, giving approximately $h - p - q$. For SARIMA models, the seasonal AR and MA parameters are also included, giving approximately

$$
h - (p + q + P + Q).
$$

In this study, the Ljung-Box test was applied at lag $h = 24$, and models were treated as viable if the Ljung-Box $p$-value was greater than 0.05.

## A.6 Akaike Information Criterion and AICc

The Akaike Information Criterion is defined as

$$
\mathrm{AIC} = -2\log L + 2k,
$$

where $L$ is the maximised likelihood and $k$ is the number of estimated parameters. Lower AIC values indicate a better balance between model fit and model complexity.

The corrected AIC, or AICc, is used for smaller samples and is defined as

$$
\mathrm{AICc} = \mathrm{AIC} + \frac{2k(k+1)}{n-k-1},
$$

where $n$ is the training sample size. AICc was reported because the training set only contained 53 observations, and some candidate models had relatively high parameter counts.

## A.7 Forecast Accuracy Metric

Root Mean Squared Error was used to compare forecast accuracy over the 2013--2024 test period:

$$
\mathrm{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{h}\sum_{t=1}^{h}(y_t - \hat{y}_t)^2},
$$

where $y_t$ is the observed value, $\hat{y}_t$ is the forecast value, and $h = 12$ is the forecast horizon. RMSE is useful because it is reported in the same units as the original data.

## A.8 Prediction Intervals

Prediction intervals were constructed on the log scale using the forecast standard errors returned by the fitted model:

$$
\hat{y}_t^{\log} \pm z\cdot \mathrm{SE}(\hat{y}_t^{\log}).
$$

The 80% and 95% prediction intervals used the normal critical values $z = 1.282$ and $z = 1.960$, respectively. The upper and lower bounds were then back-transformed to the original scale using the exponential function.
